summary.category: Physical Nature and Characteristics of Matter
points: Matter is made up of tiny particles with spaces between them — confirmed by salt dissolving in water without raising the level, and by a few KMnO₄ crystals colouring ~1000 L of water through serial dilution.

Particles of matter are in continuous random motion (giving them kinetic energy that increases with temperature) and attract each other with forces whose strength varies between substances — maximum in solids, intermediate in liquids, minimum in gases.

Diffusion — the spontaneous intermixing of particles — occurs because of continuous particle motion and interparticle spaces; its rate increases with temperature and follows the order: gases > liquids > solids.

summary.category: States of Matter and Their Properties
points: Solids have definite shape and volume, negligible compressibility, and rigid structure because particles are tightly packed with maximum intermolecular forces and only vibrate about fixed positions.

Liquids have a fixed volume but no fixed shape (they flow and are called fluids); their particles move freely and slide over each other; gases dissolve in liquids (e.g., oxygen in water essential for aquatic life).

Gases have no fixed shape or volume, are highly compressible (enabling LPG/CNG storage), exert pressure on container walls due to fast random particle movement, and diffuse very rapidly — all because of negligible intermolecular forces and maximum interparticle spaces.

summary.category: Change of State, Latent Heat, and Evaporation
points: During a change of state (solid→liquid at melting point 273 K, liquid→gas at boiling point 373 K), temperature remains constant as heat is used to overcome forces of attraction — this hidden heat is called latent heat; steam at 373 K has more energy than water at 373 K due to the extra latent heat of vaporisation it has absorbed.

Sublimation is the direct solid→gas conversion (e.g., camphor, dry ice/solid CO₂); deposition is the reverse; the state of matter depends on both temperature AND pressure — gases can be liquefied by applying pressure and lowering temperature.

Evaporation is a surface phenomenon occurring below the boiling point; its rate increases with surface area, temperature, wind speed, and decreases with humidity; evaporation causes cooling because escaping particles absorb latent heat from the surroundings — hence cotton clothes cool the body in summer and desert coolers work best in dry weather.
